data.title: Palantir Q3 Earnings: Rule Of 40 Score Accelerates
data.description:
PLTR stock: Q3 earnings measured by the rule of 40

I last analyzed Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) on Oct. 23 with an article titled “Palantir Q3 Earnings Preview: Rethink Its DOD Reliance.” That article served as a preview for its Q3 earnings report (ER), in which I focused on PLTR's first major UK contract with the Ministry of Defence. I considered this a key milestone marking its diversification beyond U.S. government contracts and rated the stock as a hold.

Since then, the company has reported its actual earnings for Q3 on Nov. 3, 2025, as shown in the snapshot below. Therefore, this article’s goal is to examine the updates provided in Q3 and also look forward to its growth and profitability metrics in Q4. Given the high growth rate of its business, I find it very fitting to frame this examination by the rule of 40 (which will be referred to as R40 hereafter). In a nutshell, I see signs of a better/stronger combination of the company’s growth and profitability ahead, which leads me to reiterate my hold rating despite the expensive valuation.

Let me start with its Q3 ER. As seen in the next chart, PLTR delivered another terrific quarter, with all earnings metrics beating consensus. In particular, total revenue reached $1.18 billion, translating into a YOY growth of about 62.8%. Such a growth rate itself is already sufficient to push its R40 score above the threshold.

For readers new to R40, the following quotes from Wall Street Prep should provide the necessary background for this article:

The management teams of high-growth SaaS startups are often required to choose between rapid growth and expansion, or improving profitability – for which, the Rule of 40 has become a practical framework to balance the two concepts. The Rule – popularized by Brad Feld – states that a SaaS company’s revenue growth rate plus profit margin should be equal to or exceed 40%. Thus, the rule measures the balance between a SaaS company’s growth rate and profitability. Profitability can be measured by net profit, EBIT, or even EBITDA margin.

With this background, the next chart shows PLTR’s R40 score in the past 5 years since 2021 to provide a broader context. To wit, in this chart, the R40 score is defined as the summation of PLTR’s quarterly YOY growth rates of its sales and its net profit margin. As seen, PLTR’s R40 score has been on an overall upward trend. It pushed above the 40 threshold around 2024 and has been accelerating since then.

Next, I will explain why its R40 score will remain at a robust level or even further expand in the near future.

PLTR stock: Profit margin and growth outlook

Looking ahead to Q4 2025, the company is expected to release the ER on Feb. 2, 2025. If you recall from the first chart in the foregoing sector, consensus projection points to a total revenue of $1.34 billion, translating into a YOY growth of about 62.0% compared to the same period in FY 2024 (of around $827 million). Once again, the revenue growth rate itself is enough to push its R40 score well above the threshold.

To make the scorecard even stronger, PLTR’s profitability metrics are also on an expanding trajectory. As aforementioned, the application of R40 involves a certain degree of ambiguity in the choice of a profitability measure. In most cases, the further downstream of the income statement and/or cash flow statement we go (e.g., from EBITDA margin, to EBIT margin, to net profit margin, and finally free cash flow margin), the more stringent the rule becomes.

PLTR’s is by no means the “normal” case, as you can easily tell from the next chart. As seen below, its EBIT and EBITDA margins are about the same (21.8% vs. 22.5%), and both are noticeably below its net profit margin of 28.1%. This is due to PLTR’s superb scalability in my view, and I will revisit this point later. Also note how rapidly these margins have expanded in recent years. As a reflection, the 5-year averages of all these margins are negative, as seen. For Q4, based on consensus’ revenue estimate of $1.34 billion, EPS estimate of $0.23 on a normalized basis, and its current share count, the implied net margin would be 43.8%. When combined with the projected YOY revenue growth rate of 62.0%, the R40 score would be again over 100.

PLTR stock’s valuation in focus

As a downside, the terrific combination of growth and profitability comes with a hefty price tag. As you can see from the following chart, Palantir's valuation metrics collectively receive an off-putting F grade. The P/E ratios for Palantir are 266 on a TTM non-GAAP basis and 235 on a forward basis. Even the EV/Sales (on a TTM basis) is in the triple digits (102x).

These valuation metrics are not only extreme by absolute standards (say compared to the sector average of its own historical averages), but are also extreme when compared to other businesses that also pass the R40. As an example, the following McKinsey study provided statistics for the EV/sales ratio for a collection of businesses with passing R40 scores:

Data show that investors reward companies that are at or above the Rule of 40 with consistently higher enterprise value (EV) to revenue multiples. Moreover, the higher the number, the greater the gain. Top-quartile SaaS companies generate nearly three times the multiples of those in the bottom (see exhibit).

The detailed results from this report are shown in the chart below. As seen, scalable SaaS companies that can pass the R40 have enjoyed an average valuation multiple of 12x EV/sales. For those with a superbly scalable model with an R40 score in the top quartile, they were traded at a further premium of 22x EV/sales. By either standard, PLTR’s current EV/sales ratios (in the range of ~90x to ~100x on an FWD and TTM basis, respectively) are quite extreme.

Other risks and final thoughts

Back to the positives, if you recall from the data presented in a foregoing section, PLTR’s EBIT and EBITDA margins are both noticeably below its net profit margin. Furthermore, its net profit margin of 28.1% is, in turn, lower than its FCF (free cash flow) margin of 30.3%. This abnormality is a reflection of PLTR’s superbly scalable operation in my model.

To analyze a high-growth stock, investors should delineate the company’s growth CAPEX and maintenance CAPEX, which are both treated as expenses in accounting terms. For PLTR, the fact that its FCF margin is above than its net margin (which in turn is above its EBIT and EBITDA margins) is a sign that its accounting profits underestimate its true economic profits. Judging by the updates provided in Q3’s ER, I see several initiatives that could further strengthen its scalability.

In particular, I am impressed by the company’s ability to scale its AI FDEs (Forward Deployed Engineers). I consider its deep FDE expertise and Ontology implementation to be a crucial—and also winning—differentiator when compared against other competing products, which mostly provide generic and one-size-fits-most solutions in my view.

All told, this revisit to PLTR is triggered by the release of its Q3 ER and also the latest earnings revisions for Q4. The company kept demonstrating rapid growth, which makes the rule of 40 a very relevant framework to understand its growth, profitability, and valuation. In a nutshell, I see strong potential for the company to maintain or even further expand its triple-digit R40 score. Such a superb score provides a counterbalance to its extreme valuation multiples, leading to my Hold rating.

data.title: Cognex: Fairly Valued Given Limited Near-Term Visibility
data.description:
Cognex (CGNX) achieved solid growth and improved margins in the third quarter. Its business was boosted by one-off revenue, though, and hence this strength shouldn’t be extrapolated. Looking through temporary factors, Cognex's revenue is growing at a single-digit rate, and margins remain depressed due to growth investments.

I previously suggested that caution was warranted due to macro uncertainty, and Cognex's stock is roughly flat since then. While automation and AI adoption support Cognex’s long-term outlook, I continue to believe the stock is fully valued given near-term risks. I am cautious regarding the macro environment, particularly in relation to the large investments that have been made in logistics in recent years.

Market Conditions

While Cognex's revenue is rebounding, supported by acquisitions and partnership revenue, the company continues to face a muted demand environment that varies significantly across end markets.

Consumer electronics, logistics, and packaging were areas of strength in the third quarter. Consumer electronics has been aided by supply chain diversification across geographies, although this will only be a temporary tailwind. Cognex expects its consumer electronics business to return to YoY growth in 2025 for the first time since 2022.

The logistics business continues to grow at a double-digit pace, driven by large e-commerce customers. I question how long this strength will persist, though, due to pressure on consumer spending and the large investments in capacity that have been made in recent years. Cognex has stated that customer spend is primarily related to the automation of existing facilities rather than capacity expansion, though, and also believes that penetration is still low. Packaging is also underpenetrated and is a less cyclical market. Cognex’s new customer initiative is helping to drive adoption in this vertical.

Cognex’s semiconductor business is predominantly vision systems for large equipment manufacturers. This remains a somewhat variable part of the market despite the large investments being made in AI at the moment.

Automotive remains a weak spot, although Cognex believes the decline in 2025 will be less severe than 2024’s 14% contraction. Demand is variable across geographies, with America strong relative to Europe.

It is worthwhile keeping in mind that Cognex has a short-cycle business and hence has limited visibility into future demand conditions. The number of Cognex job openings has been trending down in recent months, which suggests that conditions remain challenging.

Cognex Business Updates

Cognex offers AI-enabled solutions for industrial machine vision applications, positioning the company to capitalize on rising adoption of automation and the rapidly improving capabilities of AI. While this is a large and growing opportunity, it is likely to play out over an extended period, meaning investors should not expect Cognex's growth to suddenly surge. This partially relates to the number and diversity of customers, which complicates the sales process.

Cognex's emerging customer initiative aims to double the company's customer count by driving adoption among smaller organizations. Supporting this, Cognex continues to invest in solutions that are easier to use. Little information has been given on the progress of this initiative in recent quarters.

Cognex recently introduced the SLX (Solutions Experience) product line, which is targeted at logistics. This is a portfolio of AI-enabled machine vision devices that are easy to use. Initial applications targeted by Cognex include object classification and side-by-side detection, which is useful for sorting items. This extends Cognex’s business beyond barcode reading into higher-value applications.

Cognex also recently launched OneVision, a cloud platform that helps manufacturers build and deploy AI-enabled vision tools. OneVision is still in limited release, but feedback from early adopters has been positive. It is hoped that OneVision will drive adoption and unlock new use cases.

Financial Analysis

Cognex generated $277 million in revenue in the third quarter, an increase of 18% YoY. The company benefited from a commercial partnership in Q3 that temporarily boosted revenue ($30 million), although the 2024 comparable period was difficult due to the inclusion of an extra month of revenue from Moritex ($5 million in revenue in Q3 2024). The partnership is targeting medical lab automation and was only expected to provide an $8-$14 million revenue boost in the quarter. Revenue growth excluding these impacts was 13% on a constant currency basis.

Greater China (includes Taiwan) revenue increased 9% YoY even though shifts in ordering entities and the extra month of Moritex revenue were headwinds. Strength was broad-based strength, excluding automotive, and represents a sharp shift from prior quarters. Cognex has suggested that competition has eased and that pricing is stabilizing as a result. Cognex believes its China business is supported by the investments it has made in local capabilities. It is worth noting that around three-quarters of its revenue is from multinationals operating in China rather than domestic manufacturers. Other Asia revenue increased 4% YoY in Q3, adjusting for the additional month of Moritex revenue last year, supported by the relocation of consumer electronics supply chains.

Cognex expects $230-$245 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, representing roughly 3% YoY growth at the midpoint. The company believes it is in the initial stages of an expansion cycle, although this isn't really clear. Manufacturing PMIs suggest stabilization rather than expansion at this stage, and Cognex has limited forward visibility. Customer CapEx could also easily drop from current levels if economic conditions deteriorate.

Cognex's gross margins remain under pressure due to a combination of tariffs and revenue mix. In particular, Moritex and Cognex's logistics businesses have been growth drivers recently and have lower margins. Cognex's gross margin was also hit by tariffs in the third quarter.

Cognex's adjusted EBITDA improved significantly due in large part to the high-margin revenue from the commercial partnership, along with continued cost discipline (adjusted operating expenses were only up 1% YoY). Incentive compensation was a headwind in the quarter, though. Cognex's free cash flows also continue to improve, reaching $214 million over the past 12 months.

Cognex returned $37 million to shareholders in Q3 through a combination of share repurchases and dividends. The company ended the quarter with $600 million in net cash and investments, some of which seems destined for further M&A.

Conclusion

Cognex's revenue multiple is now a little over 6x. While this may seem high for a company that is only expecting a 10% market CAGR through the cycle, there is potential for market share gains, supported by M&A. Cognex also has a demonstrated ability to generate high margins. It will likely take a number of years, but I expect the company's operating margins to eventually exceed 25% again. As a result, I think an argument can be made that the stock will provide mid- to low-teen returns over the long run.

There is also the issue of the current demand environment to consider. I see no reason to expect Automotive demand to rebound in the short term. Consumer electronics demand could also slip again once supply chain relocation efforts are finished. Cognex's business really hinges on large ongoing investments in logistics. Given the faltering state of the economy, I wouldn't rely on this part of the business continuing to generate strong growth. As a result, I am "Neutral" on Cognex's stock currently, even though the company has a bright future.

data.title: 3 Well-Positioned AI Stocks From Steven Cress
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Seeking Alpha's Head of Quant, Steve Cress on how crazy November was and why quant works (0:45). Micron and CommScope, 2 quant strong buys in AI sector (12:00). Worth buying near 52 week highs? (17:00). Seagate Technology, pick #3 (19:30). Benefits of employing this strategy (22:00). Quant performance during GFC (29:00).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: We are back with Steve Cress, our head of quant at Seeking Alpha. Always a great pleasure to have you on the podcast. Thanks for coming back on Investing Experts, Steve.

Steve Cress: Rena, thank you so much for having me. It's always a pleasure to be here. Truly appreciate it.

Rena Sherbill: Our audience truly appreciates it. I truly appreciate it. I find them personally edifying. I know the investors find your previous appearances super informative and helpful. You've recently been on talking three growth stocks, three income stocks. That's what you were talking about recently.

Before that, you updated us with some picks from the Pro Quant Portfolio and Alpha Picks. What will you be giving the people today, Steve?

Steve Cress: Well, today I want to actually go back to the three AI stocks because November was a crazy month. And rightly so during our last presentation, it was basically to have this barbell approach where we focused on three income stocks and three growth stocks.

And sure enough, there definitely was volatility during the month of November. But so crazy that the market started off strong, then it sold off, and then it started coming back.

So you're really well positioned to be in both places, taking advantage of the dip and getting some decent yields on those three income stocks, because those prices have since appreciated. So the yields are not great as high as they were.

And with our three AI stocks, the AI stocks have started to come back. The technologies arena and sector are moving. But these stocks, we still think are really strong in terms of their fundamentals, not overvalued. And that's why we think they're really well positioned.

So I think before we jump into it, I know this is one of your favorite parts of every presentation.

Rena Sherbill: Yeah, people are gonna be really excited. It's disclaimer time, everybody's favorite time. I'm gonna read a disclaimer really quickly and then we're gonna get to the heart of the matter:

We are not advising you personally concerning the nature of potential value or suitability of any particular security. You alone are solely responsible for determining whether any investment security strategy or any product or service is appropriate or suitable for you based on your investment objectives and personal and financial situation. This presentation is for information purposes only.

Content is presented as of the day published or indicated and may be superseded by future events. It represents Steve's opinions, which may not reflect the views of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker, US investment advisor, or investment bank.

But Steve Cress is a very smart and capable and articulate man. So let's get into it. Steve, if you want to just give a brief overview of what Quant is bringing to the table and then we can get into it.

Steve Cress: Sure. And for those who are new, I'll just give a brief background on my almost 35 years in the investment industry. The majority of my career was spent at Morgan Stanley, where I worked for 13 years. ran a proprietary trading desk in quantitative strategies. I also was the head of international at Northern Trust for investments. And I was the founder of a quant hedge fund, as well as a fintech company that was purchased by Seeking Alpha.

And that is what brought me here today. And it's been a great journey. Today in our presentation, we're gonna cover why quant pullbacks and rallies and market rotation, the case for our AI growth stocks, which we will be focusing on today.

So how do we achieve our results? What is a quant system? Well, a quant system uses data, math and algorithms to identify investment opportunities. Now that sounds pretty fancy, but at the end of the day, we really do what any investment analyst does that works at Morgan Stanley or Merrill Lynch or Goldman Sachs.

We use fundamental analysis in our quant system. And in fact, I refer to it as GARP, which is a very common investment approach. It's called growth at a reasonable price. So our quant system has an element of diversification in terms of the styles that it looks at, where we're looking for value, growth, profitability, momentum, and positive analyst revisions, which is very similar to what many analysts do.

I will say there are quant models that just focus on momentum. There are some that just focus on growth. The same way an investment manager could just focus on value. You could pick a particular style, but we pick a diverse approach because inherently I believe that diversification will maximize your returns and minimize your risk over a period of time. So we employ basically this fundamental strategy that uses GARP.

However, what makes it quantity is the power of computer processing. So any individual analyst when they cover a set of companies, their bandwidth is maybe to cover 15 or 20 stocks overall. Because of the power of computer processing, we can cover about 5000 stocks, and we can actually provide a fresh directional recommendation every day. So imagine an analyst just updating their research every single day, that doesn't really happen and take some time to update their research reports.

I wouldn't say issue a report. It could be another month or two before they write another report on it. With our quant system, basically every day we go over every company's income statement, cash flow statement, balance sheet, and hundreds of financial metrics, and we score it all on a relative basis.

By scoring it on a relative basis, we could look at a company versus its sector and help identify the strong companies from the weak companies. That is the quant aspect. is that power of computer processing, but we do it with the underlying investment approach being fundamentals.

And we have a good track record too. This shows our simulated trades over a five year period. This actually is not a back test. This is a simulated trades. It's been in existence here at Seeking Alpha since this time. And you could see what we do here is we take all our quant strong buys.

So on any given day, there could be 350 to 400 strong buys, the portfolio of a stock is no longer strong buy comes out of the portfolio that comes back to strong buy goes back in and we do the same with our measurement with Wall Street analysts and then as a benchmark we use the S&P 500 and you can see over a five-year period the sinking out of a quant system is up 227% for its strong buys versus Wall Street strong buys up only 33% and the S&P 500 up 66% for the same period so you can see we have a really good track record using our quantitative approach.

Rena Sherbill: I just want to say for people's edification, you can find that at seekingalpha.com/performance/quant to see for yourself the great performance that quant has had.

Steve Cress: Thank you so much. Appreciate it.

So a little bit about the market pullback and rally. This is very short term oriented because as many investors were aware, the beginning of the year was actually quite tough. The market did have a big correction and then all hats were off and the rally came.

And really up until the October period, we were seeing new highs with the S&P 500 (SP500) and the NASDAQ (NDAQ) get into November and the beginning, looked good. And then it took a nosedive. What I'm going to do is go to a chart just to show people what the market looks like. (SPY), pull that up. And as I was saying, really up until October, we had a great rally from April.

Of course we had a major hiccup as the tariffs were introduced. Then we were off to the races, get to October. Then when you look at the one month view, you can see where the market started and basically had a little correction, started to come back up, the correction basically lost confidence and it started plummeting down.

So it's been a crazy November. And then we got to the point where the market really sort of discounted what was happening with the Fed, with interest rates, the possibility that they could be stagnant, that there could be no change, and then the possibility that there still might be a change because of labor data. So the market really sort of discounted everything in.

And I was also very fearful during this period that AI and technology stocks were overvalued. They came down, investors felt comfortable with it. And right around November 21st, the market started to pick up steam again to our current level, where we're at about 682 right now from a low of about 652 just a little over two weeks ago.

So just as a recap, market started fairly strong, started to lose faith of investors, last push to get it back up and then totally investors just like got really scared of the AI bubble. What was going to happen with interest rates?

And we took a, not quite a nosedive, but a pretty hard fall from 683 all the way down to about 652. And here we are back up at 682 now. So the market has basically discounted the concerns, felt a lot of the AI and technology stocks were oversold, and it has come right back. So that brings us to where we are now.

Interesting enough, volatility has really started to spike again. And obviously with a lot of the concerns I just mentioned with the government shutdown, with AI stocks being overbought, so market came off and volatility really had spiked. And here we could sort of take a look at the performance of each sector.

So I have this sorted by the five day performance over here. And you can see that the mega cap tech stocks that previously led the rally had really sold off. The way we can look at that is if you look at the one month on the far right, you can see the one month data point shows technology down 4.77 % and it had been leading.

And then if we go to the bottom, you can see the healthcare stocks were the best performing stock over the last four weeks. And if we look at consumer staples, you can see that was the second best performing sector.

However, that's over the course of the month. And now that we've had the rally back, you can see the sectors that were stronger over the month, the safe havens are now showing lower performance.

Now we have technology has been doing well for the last five days. We have the energy sector has really picked up over last five days, up 2.28%. Utilities and healthcare sector are down over the last five days. So giving up some of that momentum that they had built.

And again, a couple of weeks ago, tech was down, people were scared, it was in focus. You can see AI bubble fears in the spotlight via Jim Cramer. And now the stocks have really started to come back.

And indeed, you can see interest rate traders who were really fearful at that time that there might be only a 50% chance of a cut. Now today, 90% of traders expect a rate cut in December. And if you looked at this a couple of weeks ago, was that 50-50 and if you looked at it from November 6, you could see that 70% of the traders expected a cut.

So from 70% of traders to about 50% of traders and now 90% of traders expect a cut. So it is just all over the place.

The market is incredibly uncertain and professional interest rate traders on November 6th are saying, November 6th, 70% of them expecting a cut. Then like a week ago, it was 50-50 and now today 90% of the traders are expecting a cut.

And what we could see too, this is fairly interesting, the market had sold off to a point where the CNN fear and greed index was an extreme fear. And I think it was right around that time that I presented Rena and I said, for me, historically, the longer you say an extreme fear, typically the better buying opportunity it is because the name of the game at the end of the day is buy low sell high. And markets tend to go a lot lower when you're in extreme fear. So that's the time you really want to be buying.

So we've actually moved up today. You could see a CNN score of 24. So even though we're still in extreme fear, you could see we're coming off the bottom. So it's showing that the market is starting to feel a bit more comfortable. And certainly the last couple of days, we've seen the market trading better.

So without further ado, let's go into our first stock, Micron Technology, (MU). Market cap on it is around 271 billion. This is a quant strong buy in information technology, it ranks one out of 538 stocks. Within the semiconductors industry, it ranks one out of 67.

It is the only US-based manufacturer of advanced memory chips with leading edge technologies. Many of you are probably familiar with it because it is a really big company.

What I want to highlight here is you can see this stock actually really started to sell off before many more and it came down much harder. And I'm so pleased that we recommended it with our barbell strategy right around that time.

So you can see the stock has done well, but I still expect it to do continue to trade well. And you'll see there is a quant strong buy. And below that, you'll see the factor grades. And remember, these grades are all to the sector. So when you see a valuation for B, that is that means it's very attractive compared to the sector.

But despite the run up in the stock, since mid November, the valuation is the same that it was three months ago. But more importantly, if you look at where it was six months ago, it was a D minus. So it was actually considered fairly overvalued at that point. And now it's far more attractive in terms of its valuation framework.

We'll click into valuation, you can see some of the grades and here you'd see this is you know you typically typically don't see this for an IT stock but the 4p on this is 15 times versus the sector 30 times so this is a 50 discount to the sector on a PE basis hence why the stock has such a great overall valuation grade the PEG which is a combination of growth and PE is an A plus at 0.03 versus the sector at 0.93 so it's at a 96 % discount to the sector.

Valuation framework looks incredible. If we click on growth, looks like almost a straight A report card here. If you're looking at revenue growth, forward revenue growth is 36 % versus the sector at almost 8%. If you look at EPS growth on a forward basis, it's got 191 % growth rate versus the sector at 10%.

So really like Micron Technology (MU), they're also extremely profitable by clicking the profitability grades. You could see the return on equity is 17%. So it gets an A minus versus a sector at 6%. So the point of these letter grades is to really give you an instant characterization of where it compares to the sector. So you don't even have to really look at the data. You just know by looking at the grade that it's strong on profitability or the underlying metric return on equity as much stronger than the group, but we're also very transparent.

So we provide the actual data point as well. So for Micron Technology, we go to the analyst revisions grade, we will see in the last 90 days, 29 analysts have actually moved their estimates up and zero have moved it down. And that's for the full year estimate for the quarter, 24 analysts have moved their earnings estimates up and no analysts have moved their earnings estimates down.

So analysts showing that they're really confident that this company will continue to do well and putting their money where their mouth is by upgrading the earnings estimates for the company.

Our number two stock is CommScope Holding (COMM). This is much smaller. This would be considered a mid-cap company with a market cap of about $3.66 billion.

This is also an IT stock. It ranks number two in the sector, so right behind Micron, out of 538 stocks. And within its industry of communications equipment, it actually ranks one out of 39. And they are a global leader in providing infrastructure solutions for communications, data centers, and entertainment networks. And that data center aspect is why this stock has been red hot this year, doing incredibly well. On a PEG basis, it's at a 51% discount to the sector.

They have an enormous cash amount per share at $3.18 per share. It's about 42 % greater than the sector. Very positive in terms of analyst revisions. There's been four up revisions and zero down. And it has an EBITDA margin of 23 % and a lever-free cash flow margin of 28%. So again, looking really, really strong. And if you look at the profitability grade, you could see the profitability grade is an A and six months ago it was a C plus.

So the profitability has actually improved tremendously for the company. Growth has come down a little bit from an A to B minus, but still overall very strong and analyst revisions very strong for the stock as well. And if we click on that, we'll see that over the last 90 days.

Rena Sherbill:By the way, while you're getting there, if I can throw out a question. One of the questions you released in an article on this topic, a little bit different, but on this topic on seeking health. And one of the questions was about some of these stocks being near their 52 week highs. If you want to bring that context into conversation, I think that would be helpful.

Steve Cress: Yeah, no, absolutely. It's really important as you're looking at this. So I'll just say this has four upward revisions and no downward revisions in terms of earnings estimates from analysts. So very positive.

But answering Rena's question in terms of these stocks running up, you can see this is up 303 % in the last year. That's a massive run near the 52 week high. A lot of people typically are afraid to do that, you can't make investment decision based on how much a stock is going to move.

I mean, thinking all you would have missed on Amazon (AMZN) or Apple (AAPL) or Meta (META), had you said 10 years ago, the stock was near a 52 week high, you would miss out on incredible performance over the following 10 years. So what's really to focus on is what I consider the key elements of investing are value, growth and profitability and looking at those metrics versus the sector.

And that's exactly what we do. So you can see here the valuation, despite the stock being up 303 % in the last year, the valuation rate is a B plus versus A plus six months ago. So it's a little bit more expensive, but for a stock that's up 300%, that's still a much better value versus the rest of the sector. The growth has come down a little bit, but it's still B minus.

The profitability is huge. So, the profitability is really moving up for the company. And again, you want to focus on where this company is versus the sector. Not so much, has the stock moved up 100 % or 300 % or 500%. If it's still good value versus the sector and it still has better growth in the sector, that's the stock that you want. You don't want to buy it just because it's on a 52-week low.

And in fact, if you bought a basket of stocks that were on a 52-week low, versus a basket of stocks at a 52 week high and held it for a year, you would have made much more money by investing the stocks that were at a 52 week high.

Because typically if stocks are near a 52 week low, something is either wrong with the industry or with management or with the company, and that's why the stock has traded off. That does not mean it's a buying opportunity. So that is CommScope.

So we can go right to Seagate (STX). Again, this stock has done fairly well year to date. It's up 212% the last six months, it's up 126%. And you can see the valuation has come off a bit. It's not quite where it was six months ago, but it's still in line with the sector. But look at the growth, the growth for this company is an A plus versus the sector.

data.title: Strategy Dilutes Its Equity As Bitcoin Crashes: A Major Red Flag
data.description:
Strategy Inc (MSTR) stock is in a confirmed bear market this year, having fallen 37% in price, while the S&P 500 rose 15.5%. The main cause of the selloff was obvious enough: Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fell in price, while making up over 90% of Strategy’s balance sheet assets. It’s understandable that a stock would come down in price after the asset it holds comes down in price; in Strategy’s case, the passively owned asset is worth orders of magnitude more than the entire operating business. Strategy has further borrowed extensively in order to acquire its Bitcoin position. It’s for this reason that many investors see Strategy as a Bitcoin proxy, akin to a leveraged BTC ETF.

So, the main reason for Strategy’s poor performance this year is obvious enough. However, there are other things under the hood too. In addition to having bought up an asset that has performed poorly this year, Strategy has also diluted its equity extensively. At the end of 2024, Strategy had 192.5 million shares outstanding. As of this year’s most recent report, the company had 283.5 million shares outstanding. So, in under a year, we have seen Strategy increase its share count by 47%, reducing each shareholder’s equity interest to a considerable degree. Bitcoin would need to rise at least 47% in price to overcome the effect of this dilution; in light of this, it’s somewhat surprising that MSTR is down just 38% this year.

Dilution is not something new for Strategy. In dilution to having diluted its equity this year, Strategy has done the same over the last five years, nearly tripling its share count in that period. At the end of 2020, the company had 96.8 million shares outstanding, which rose to 283.5 million by last quarter. So, the company has increased its share count by 194.7% over five years.

The dilution issue doesn’t stop there, either. Strategy has financed its Bitcoin purchases largely with convertible debt, a type of debt that offers holders an option to exchange their shares for equity, in exchange for them receiving a lower interest rate. As a result of using this type of debt, Strategy has somewhat lower interest expenses than you’d think, given its amount of debt ($70.4 million in interest on $8.2 billion worth of debt). The downside of this arrangement is that debt holders have the right to exchange their bonds for equity, so there is even more dilution potentially coming in the future.

Put simply, Strategy is an unsound financial house that can only keep itself going if Bitcoin continues rising in price extremely rapidly. This year, with Bitcoin having fallen 1.8% in price, "rapid price appreciation" is the furthest thing from what's actually being observed. When I started writing this article, Bitcoin was down 8% for the year, but it regained lost ground in an overnight rally. If BTC had remained down 8% then the dilution would have been a major issue; it will become a major issue again if BTC once again starts falling. It is already an issue now, from a value perspective: Bitcoin has not risen anywhere near 47% in value since MSTR equity was diluted by 47%; in fact, it has fallen in value slightly. This is the reason for MSTR’s poor stock price performance.

However, if Bitcoin starts really crashing, like it did in 2018, then actual threats to MSTR’s financial soundness could emerge. Strategy has $70.4 million in annual interest expenses and a free cash flow negative operating business. The company's Bitcoin holdings are estimated to be worth $55.9 billion. If Bitcoin crashes 90%, these holdings fall to $5.59 billion in value, and suddenly $70.4 million in interest expenses starts looking like a big deal. In such a scenario, the company has to sell of 1.2% of its Bitcoin each year to pay interest, and that's not to mention other operating expenses. The end result could be the company chipping away at its assets while its debts continue having to be paid. That combined with dilution could deliver a truly dismal result to shareholders.

When I last covered Strategy, I rated it a sell on the grounds that it was exposed to Bitcoin’s volatility in a highly leveraged package, while having none of Bitcoin’s practical use cases. I still stand by the essence of what I wrote in that article, which was a success, as MSTR fell 45% in price after it published. Now, however, we have MSTR trading at a much lower price than it did when I last wrote about it. Those who shorted the stock when my sell rating was published, now have an excellent opportunity to take profits. While I’d still rather sell MSTR than own it, I think the easy money has been made shorting MSTR. Accordingly, I’m upgrading my rating on the stock to hold. In the ensuing paragraphs, I will explain why I made that decision.

The Bear Case On MSTR

A bear case on MSTR can be built on its leveraged exposure to Bitcoin, heavy dilution, and high level of convertible debt.

First, let’s take a look at Strategy’s Bitcoin exposure. It’s questionable why a corporation would choose to serve as a proxy for a cryptocurrency. Unlike an ETF, a company has to pay taxes whenever it earns profit selling Bitcoin. Currently, Strategy’s position is that it won’t sell its Bitcoin, but it does not earn enough money from its operating business to pay its interest (at least not most years). So, it may be forced to sell its Bitcoin in order to meet obligations. Currently, its approach is to borrow more money in order to service the interest on previous borrowings. This is likely unsustainable–if I were a gambling man, I’d bet on Strategy being forced to sell Bitcoin sooner rather than later.

Now, if Strategy ever is forced to sell its Bitcoin, its disadvantage compared to a Bitcoin ETF will become evident: the company will have to pay taxes. Potentially, large ones: short term capital gains are taxed at 21% federally. Additionally, when you play Bitcoin through MSTR, you forego the practical use cases of Bitcoin; i.e. spending it and using it for remittances. Given its disadvantages compared to both ETFs and direct Bitcoin holdings, it’s not clear what the “purpose” of holding Strategy as a Bitcoin proxy is.

Next up, we have Strategy’s heavy amounts of leverage. According to Seeking Alpha Quant, MSTR has $8.2 billion worth of debt, incurring $70.4 million in annual interest expenses. The interest rate implied by these figures is 0.85%, which is rather low. However, there are issues with the way that MSTR achieves its low-interest rate. It does so by issuing convertible debt; i.e., debt that can be converted into MSTR stock. Such debts usually have lower interest rates because the conversion option is perceived as being valuable. However, they create even more dilution if bondholders choose to exercise their options. As I showed previously, Bitcoin needs to rise at least 47% to make up for the dilution in Strategy stock observed in the last year. If more bondholders convert their bonds, then MSTR will have to rise more than 47%. That’s not the kind of thing that a risk-averse investor tends to like betting on.

Last but not least, there’s the multiples that Strategy trades at. The company has negative cash flows, so many valuation multiples are unavailable to us. However, it scores extremely poorly in terms of EV/sales, price/sales and EV/EBITDA. These “EV” multiples are ones that take into account Strategy’s debt, indicating that the stock is expensive when factoring in the claims by all financial interests in the underlying company.

Why I Wouldn't Go Short Here

As you can likely tell by the previous paragraphs, I take a fairly dim view of Strategy’s financial prospects. Indeed, I would rather short Strategy than buy it, if someone put a gun to my head and forced me to choose. But in reality, I think that at this point, MSTR stock is better off avoided than shorted. I’d advise anybody who shorted Strategy around the time my previous article went live, to take profits now.

Why?

There are a few reasons. First, Bitcoin is currently near the lower end of its 52-week range. Going by history, the expectation would be for the cryptocurrency to recover from here, not fall further. That’s no guarantee; it’s just what price momentum from the recent past would predict. Bitcoin certainly could fall further from here, but I have no reason to think that it will.

Second, my previous Strategy call (a sell call on September of 2025) was quite successful, and anybody who traded based on it (i.e. shorted Strategy) and did not sell until today, would be sitting on a large, market beating gain. It would be prudent to take those profits, given the unpredictable nature of Bitcoin price fluctuations.

Third and finally, Strategy does have some things going for it, such as the ability to obtain debt financing ‘cheaply’ (considering interest rates alone). This financing isn't cheap from the perspective of shareholders, who face more dilution from the convertible bonds; but it is cheap from management's perspective, as it does not require them to pay significant interest. So the company can probably keep kicking the can down the road for a surprisingly long time.

The Bottom Line

Taking everything covered in this article into account, I think that Strategy is a stock best avoided. Having come down in price by nearly 50%, it’s no longer the obvious short it once was. But it’s not a buy either. Investors considering any position in MSTR would be better off thinking about other opportunities.

Introduction

As we wind down 2025, I am already thinking about 2026 and what the market's future holds. I will say now that we are exiting a short, favorable period and entering an unfavorable period that will last through the end of 2031. I base these beliefs on historical cycles that I have been studying for the past several years. I have already analyzed the Benner Cycle, in which I stated that 2026 would mark the end of a favorable period, and that we will enter an unfavorable period during 2027-2032. I have also performed extensive analysis of the 7-year cycle (Shmita) and will revisit it here.

The Seven-Year Cycle and The Sabbatical Year

The seven-year cycle, also called the Shmita, is based on the belief that every seven years, one’s fields should go fallow. There is compelling evidence that this cycle also affects the stock market, and I have written about it a few times. The thesis is that the stock market average returns are positive in 6 out of 7 years. It is during the seventh year, or sabbatical year, that the market averages a loss. Since 1793, we have had 33 of these seven-year cycles. This is how each of the cycles has performed during all of our stock market years:

Simon Kuznets theorized that economic cycles run in 15–25-year cycles. The cycles are determined by shifts in the economy, such as industrialization, technological advances, and demographic changes. Using sinusoidal models, I believe that 2026 will see mundane returns, with the stock market posting positive gains but performing below the historical average. After 2026, during 2027-2031, the picture gets scarier, with returns potentially falling below the historical 25th percentile. If I had to guess, there is a 24% chance of a down market next year, and those probabilities will increase in the subsequent years.

My model seeks to identify calendar years in which conditions are more or less favorable for an equity investor. My model says that 2026-2031 will be unfavorable, and conditions will improve during 2032-2035. Based on this, I see the market growing at 7% for 2026, but that is with a lot of volatility (+/-14%), and the market has a worse-than-average chance of seeing potential losses. If I were to rate SPY, I would give it a "Hold" rating.

So, what should I do? If the market were in a favorable environment with low inflation, I would allocate 19% to cash and cash equivalents, 25% to fixed income, 42% to equities, and 13% to alternative investments, including real estate, gold, and hedge funds. If I were anticipating a market downturn, my portfolio would shift to 17% cash and cash equivalents, 32% fixed income, 38% equities, and 13% in alternative investments.

This is a strategic approach. Because of the margins of error, one can never be 100% certain of market behavior. What they can be sure of is that it is prudent to design a well-allocated portfolio than can respond to market conditions.

Good luck and have a great 2026.

data.title: Asia 2026: 5 Questions For Japan's Year Of 'Sanaenomics'
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

What are the main reasons for economic recovery in 2026?

  • Japan’s economy is expected to recover quite meaningfully in 2026 thanks to 'Sanaenomics' and reduced trade tensions. A lower cost of living and solid wage growth are expected to support private spending, while easing trade tensions should boost exports. Combined with structural labour shortages at home, robust global AI demand, reduced US tariff uncertainty, and solid corporate earnings, we expect significant investment growth in 2026. For wage growth, Rengo, one of the largest trade unions in Japan, is vying for pay raises of at least 5% during the upcoming spring wage negotiations. We expect another near-5% wage growth rise for 2026, which will eventually turn real wages positive.
  • Like other major Asian tech exporters, strong demand for semiconductors is expected to drive export growth. In addition, following the US-Japan trade agreement, auto exports have started to recover. We believe the weak yen has helped offset some of the effects of tariffs, which will continue to benefit Japanese exporters.
  • The Bank of Japan’s slow and steady normalisation process will continue, but real interest rates will remain negative. Accommodative monetary conditions will therefore support growth.

Semiconductors will boost exports, and US exports should rebound

Will the BoJ continue to hike rates?

  • The BoJ continued its move toward policy normalisation in 2025, implementing a 25bp hike in the first quarter and pursuing quantitative tightening by reducing its holdings of Japanese government bonds (JGBs) and ETF/J-REITs. Given recent hawkish remarks from several BoJ officials, including Governor Kazuo Ueda, the likelihood of another rate hike in December appears high.
  • Growth conditions are projected to improve in 2026, with core inflation remaining above 2%. This may prompt the BoJ to consider a 25bp hike, with the policy rate reaching 1.0%. The BoJ will monitor whether wage growth remains above 3% in the spring negotiations and whether underlying inflation remains near 2%. Policy normalisation will likely proceed slowly due to policy coordination with the government. We expect the next hike to materialise much later, in October. With real interest rates still negative, a 25bp hike is justifiable in our view.
  • However, there is potential for the BoJ to raise rates to a total of 50bp, depending on wage growth and the strength of private consumption. This could be triggered, ironically, by Sanaenomics.

Core inflation excluding fresh food and energy will stay well above 2%, supporting the BoJ's rate hike

How might Sanaenomics affect the economy and monetary policy?

  • In early December, the Japanese cabinet approved the government’s first economic stimulus package, valued at approximately ¥21.3tr. The initiatives are focused on stabilising inflation, enhancing defence and diplomacy, and promoting sustainable growth.
  • We agree with the need for a stimulus package to boost short-term growth and reduce inflation, but this may influence the BoJ’s monetary policy.
  • Inflation is projected to drop below 2% when energy subsidies are introduced early next year, with an expected decrease to 1.4% year-on-year in 2026 from 3.1% in 2025. Lower energy and food costs, financial support for childcare, and a strong equity market should stimulate more consumer spending, which may result in stronger growth than anticipated. This may also push up core inflation above 2%.So far, BoJ policymakers have suggested that current inflation is mainly driven by cost-push factors, meaning the underlying rate of increase hasn't yet reached 2%. Should core inflation continue to exceed headline inflation, it would indicate demand-driven inflationary conditions. In turn, the BoJ may implement more rate hikes or introduce them earlier than anticipated.

Sanaenomics might boost growth more than expected

What might Sanaenomics mean for financial markets?

  • The recent sell-off in JGBs was influenced by multiple factors, including high odds for a BoJ rate hike in December, increased debt issuance thanks to stimulus packages, and the possible unwinding of carry trades. As is widely known, Sanaenomics is based on aggressive fiscal spending to revitalise the economy. However, combined with the ongoing BoJ’s quantitative tightening, market concerns about fiscal sustainability could increase. This could boost JGB yields and weaken the JPY at the same time.
  • If yields on short-term JGBs increase rapidly, it could place a strain on households and businesses. Long-term JGBs are primarily traded by institutional investors and have a limited direct connection to the real economy. However, any sudden surge in short-term JGB yields might negatively impact the fragile economic recovery.
  • If the economy experiences a downturn, the BoJ may encounter difficulties. A sudden policy change could increase market volatility and damage overall conditions. As a result, this might prevent the BoJ from responding promptly to changes in macroeconomic conditions.

Will tensions between Japan and China increase?

  • Other key aspects of Sanaenomics include strengthening defence and diplomatic capabilities. If not implemented carefully, this policy may lead to increased regional tensions, as observed between Japan and China. We don’t think the tensions will escalate further for now. Since her conversation with US President Donald Trump, Takaichi has not commented further on China or Taiwan, suggesting the status quo will hold. With strong approval ratings, Takaichi has little incentive to provoke China.
  • Any deterioration in relations would affect Japan more than China. For example, after Japan purchased the disputed Senkaku Islands in 2012, tensions led to a 40% drop in Chinese tourism to Japan. This time, the Chinese government has cancelled flights and banned Japanese seafood in retaliation. While it's hard to gauge the full impact on Japan's economy, these actions pose clear short-term downside risks.

We expect to see a decline of Chinese tourists to Japan

data.title: What OpenAI's 'Code Red' Means For The Markets
data.description:
One of the biggest stories so far in December is Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI, just declaring a 'code red,' which has gotten outsized coverage in the financial press. It was his call to get the company more intently refocused on improving the capabilities of ChatGPT given slowing subscriptions, intensifying competition, as well as surging investment costs.

Since the launch of ChatGPT almost exactly three years ago, OpenAI has become one of the fastest-growing commercial concerns in the world, with revenues currently tracking to a $20 billion annual run rate. More than 800 million people around the globe use ChatGPT on a daily basis.

However, the company is facing growing competition from the likes of Alphabet (GOOG) and Anthropic, which may beat OpenAI on the IPO front. In late October, Microsoft (MSFT) and OpenAI announced a restructuring deal that now allows OpenAI to transition away from its nonprofit roots and likely go public. Mr. Altman just declared OpenAI will now be pulling back on investments in areas like health, shopping, and advertising. It will now be more intently focused on prioritizing improving ChatGPT capabilities and will be having daily calls around this effort.

This makes sense as Claude chatbot maker Anthropic continues to make inroads in the business community, and the recent release of Google’s Gemini 3 model last month has helped it add over 200 million users over the past three months. Gemini 3 has also outperformed ChatGPT on some industry benchmark tests. The new upgrade has garnered notable praise and buzz on social media as well. Of note, Gemini crossed 450 million monthly active users in July of this year. ChatGPT is no longer the only game in town.

OpenAI's challenges, if not addressed successfully, could undermine the entire AI Narrative and ecosystem. This is critical to the market, as most of the equity gains over the past three years have been driven by AI-related megacaps like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Microsoft. The Magnificent Seven now accounts for nearly 40% of overall market capitalization.

If OpenAI growth becomes challenged in any way, it could become a massive headwind for the overall market. That is because OpenAI is one of the key 'jenga' pieces within the Vendor-Financing-Circle that is akin to the vendor financing offered by the likes of Cisco Networks (CSCO), Lucent, and others near the end of the Internet Boom a quarter century ago. But on steroids.

Despite bleeding cash and with approximate revenues of $14 billion in FY2025, OpenAI has made just over $1.4 trillion in commitments over the next eight years for future computing power. Fortune recently projected that OpenAI will not even be profitable in FY2030 and will have to come up with over $200 billion in additional capital over that time to fund current commitments. OpenAI's capital budget is expected to grow to $17 million in FY2026, $35 billion in FY2027, and $45 billion in FY2028.

If the funding spigot dries up for any reason, numerous major players in the AI ecosystem could take major hits. Some of the most significant agreements that OpenAI has in place are highlighted below. During the summer, OpenAI and NVIDIA announced a massive AI partnership.

However, NVIDIA's CFO just admitted this 'deal' is still at the 'letter of intent' stage. In mid-September, OpenAI struck a deal with Oracle (ORCL) to purchase $300 billion in computing power over five years. This is capacity that Oracle does not currently have but is in the process of building, partially financed by debt. In fact, Meta Platforms (META), Google, Amazon, and Oracle have added just over $200 billion in debt so far in 2025, which includes off-balance sheet financing. This is more than these four entities have added in debt over the past seven years combined, to put it in perspective.

Finally, one month ago, OpenAI inked a seven-year, $38 billion deal to buy cloud services from Amazon (AMZN).

OpenAI is hardly the only thing true believers in the AI Revolution need to worry about. Based on current projections, the tech giants driving the AI Revolution will have accumulated $2.5 trillion of AI-related assets by 2030. On a five-year depreciation schedule, this would mean $500 billion in annual depreciation cost. This would be more than the combined profits of these AI juggernauts in FY2025. What possibly could go wrong? And that is without getting into whether AI GPUs will actually have a five-year useful life, instead of the historical two to three years of previous chip generations before having to be replaced.

OpenAI's 'code red' is just the latest AI development prudent investors need to keep a close eye on. Anything that disrupts, postpones, or cancels OpenAI's future commitments could undermine the entire AI Narrative.

Given current valuation levels, this also could be a huge negative for the overall market as we head into 2026.

data.title: Beyond The Hype: The Silent Stability Beneath Market Speculation
data.description:
Markets have come under pressure lately as investors worry about potential overvaluation of AI investment returns and other speculative investments. Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist with TD Wealth, says the fundamental market drivers remain intact and could reward diversified investors.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: Markets have been choppy in recent trading. But according to our featured guest today, what we're seeing may not actually be a correction. Joining us now to discuss is Brad Simpson, Chief Market Strategist with TD Wealth.

And Brad, welcome to the show.

Brad Simpson: Great to be here.

Anthony Okolie Well, as I mentioned, we've seen a lot of volatility in markets recently. But you say that this is not a correction, but more of an unwinding. What's been happening there?

Brad Simpson: Yeah, let's start with, I think, we look into your introduction. If you axed out Canada, as soon as you went to the United States, you went to technology, update in technology, update in NASDAQ, update in technology names. And if you look at the correction that we've had here, if we back up a little bit and look at what it is I and my team do at the investment office here at TD Wealth, is we are the purveyors of building and managing diversified investment portfolios.

So, in the last few weeks, I was kind of sitting back and going, there's so much discussion around correction. I don't see a correction. I don't see them across our models. I don't see them across our portfolios. And what gives?

And so, I really started to dig into it, and of course I see the technology and the technology movement. But the reality is, this unwinding we're talking about is what I would call a massive retail unwind of the technology trade. And folks that are overlevered, speculators into Bitcoin and anything that is an AI or an AI beneficiary, that's what we're seeing.

If we were looking at what this correction is not, is would be, first of all, we don't have any early signs of a recession going on right now. We don't have a break in liquidity-- there's lots of it inside the market. We're not in the middle of a credit crisis. We are not in the middle of seeing a reversal of CapEx spending. And ultimately, as we have seen the earnings season we just went through, we don't have deteriorating earnings either. In fact, we're in an incredibly buoyant time for earnings.

So I think if you look at it in those terms, is that what we're seeing is-- again, to hit this home-- is there is this speculative phase that's going on in this market, and this speculative phase is folks are discovering that if you're really using a lot of leverage, and you're chasing one sector of the market, and there's a lot of froth in that market, the net result is-- and I think we're looking up here. We're looking at here the run-up in Bitcoin and the run-up of-- we're looking at a NASDAQ. So NASDAQ, 3 times levered NASDAQ, and looking at the run-up and looking at the drop-off here.

And on the bottom line, we're looking at the S&P 500. And we're looking at margin debt and margin calls. And all of that is, I would argue, is first and foremost, the good news is it's very orderly, and it's in a concentrated part of the market. But I think you certainly, certainly could not call this a major market correction in any way, shape, or form.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. Now, you identified 2 stabilizers there for the markets: liquidity and earnings power. Are they still intact?

Brad Simpson: Yeah. Look, the-- and I should probably say that on my way over here, we've published our monthly perspectives, and our monthly perspectives is called Gravity Always Wins. And much of what we're hearing is coming from there. And so, for folks who want to read it early, when it comes out you can go to my LinkedIn, and you'll find it there.

And so, if you look at it at an environment like this one, what ends a market and what ultimately leads to a market correction is either two things. One is you look at liquidity in the system. And when we look at the system today, there's ample liquidity. In fact, I think if you went back over the last couple of months, and if you said inside the major part of the indices, where we're seeing the hyperscalers go through a correction, you could actually argue it's been a very orderly correction that we're going through there.

And the starting point to that is that we can look at something in the central bank in the US and look at their net margin levels. And if we look at the net margin levels in going through September, October, and November, money was tightening up inside of there. And as that money has tightened up a little bit, we saw a little bit of a price correction in the overall market as a whole.

When we look forward to going into December, we know all the discussion around the central banks and the US saying that there may be a drop of interest rates by 25 basis points in December. Even if there's not, they've well telegraphed there's three coming, either in December-- one in December, and then two more next year or three next year. And they've also been telegraphing that they're going to be loosening up on the quantitative tightening.

All of that points to more liquidity coming. And I think if you look at the massive fiscal spend that is out there in the US, if we go into 2026 and when we start seeing those tax cuts from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act start to come out, ample liquidity in the system and more of it coming.

And then, on the other side, when we look at earnings growth, if you look at the S&P 500 for 2026, roughly you're looking at earnings expectations of let's call it somewhere around 13%. You go over into Canada, you're somewhere around 10%. You go into Europe, you're somewhere between 8% and 9%. That's a really good, consistent earnings story that we're looking and talking about there.

And I think that as long as there's ample liquidity in the system, and on the other side, as long as there's really, really healthy earnings growth, that the likelihood of a big correction or of this market going through something devious or ugly is rather low. And I think it's important that we keep being mindful of that.

Anthony Okolie: When you talk about volatility in the markets, as you mentioned, explain to us how the rise of quant funds as well as retail investors has been driving that market that we've seen this year.

Brad Simpson: Yeah, look, that-- I don't want to lay this whole correction on the footstep of retail trading. But boy, you've got to say, if it's not the catalyst to it, it's been the facilitator of a bigger rush down.

So what we're looking at here in this chart kind of hits it all home. We have gone through a massive shift on what make markets tick over the last 4 years. So we're looking here is the investors in retail, high-frequency quant funds, and fundamental investors.

So if you look over at that 2010, how much of the market was made up of fundamental investors, look over to your far right and how much of that is now made up of fundamental investors, go back to 2010, and look at the difference between retail and high-frequency quant, and look over on the other side there.

So, I'll give you some hard numbers on this. Going into COVID, triple-levered ETF mandates or products that you could allocate to was roughly $45 billion. Fast forward today, it's about $250 billion. That $250 billion swings in the market every day. And what it's doing is, it's disproportionately having retail swing inside this market. And what we're looking here on the screen here is, (a) it's having that impact.

What we're looking at here is we're showing that liquidity flows and the GS retail favorites basket. And that is basically retail favorites basket of a lot of either tech-oriented names or companies that actually have pretty weak balance sheets but have momentum behind them and leverage behind them, and some sort of reference towards AI one way or the other, or Bitcoin-- either of those two things. And so, we see that liquidity push on it.

What we're looking there is, that's that Fed asset number I was talking about. You start to see it decline. And you start to see the retail favorites start to come off. So, retail and this absolute massive change that we've had through the use of levered products and platforms and direct platforms and all of those things have had a significant impact.

And now what we're seeing is, think about your average-- I admit, I'm a massive sports fan. I never miss a Raptors game. And every single time when I'm watching a Raptors game, and a gambling-- every five minutes, a gambling thing comes on. I must admit, I black out. I try to ignore it. The intersection between markets and gambling-- right now, we see the Commodity Exchange and negotiations, how they can link up and do business with FanDuel.

Anthony Okolie: It seems that the lines between investing and betting are blurring.

Brad Simpson: Absolutely, they are. And that is all kind of coming to the push of what we're seeing today, is that this intersection of these two are starting to have an impact.

Anthony Okolie: Is that what's driving that speculation that you talked about, and that volatility?

Brad Simpson: I think the driver between it ultimately is, one, is you know has been that as we've left COVID, we kept the foot on the pedal on keeping liquidity out there. Liquidity, as we just talked about, is one of the main drivers, right? Then you click in and you get all this new era talk, all this stuff about AI and the difference in there. Then you throw in this kind of Bitcoin story and you get those mix. So all of a sudden, you get an awful lot of people either saying, look, why should I work? Why do I need a steady paycheck? I can stay at home. There's very low volatility on this, and I can trade these things all day.

So we've seen this massive change in movement inside of the market. And I think if you add up all those pieces, that's very symbolic of the market that we have today. And so when we sat back and we looked at it is that I think, and what an awful lot of folks are discovering right now-- it's back to the article we published today, is Gravity Always Wins-- is that when leverage comes into the picture and it starts to go against you, what happens is that you start seeing a market-- and right now, it's a narrow part of the market-- where, one, liquidity starts to dry up. Two is that everybody says, well, geez, I got to sell. Everybody starts to move for the exit at the same time.

And I think that's what we're in the middle of a little bit here, anyways, in what I would call the AI beneficiaries coming to the end in the major market, but in the speculative event of this, has been a major and significant factor. We've seen roughly somewhere between $2 and $2.5 trillion wiped out in this part of the market. That's no small sum.

Anthony Okolie: Okay, so with that backdrop, what should investors be thinking about or doing right now? What should they be keeping at the top of mind right now?

Brad Simpson: Well, I think that the starting point is that, I think you can see you've got to step back a little bit. And I think you have to say and understand the difference between speculating and investing. And I'm not making this sound like speculation is all bad. It's not. If you look at-- we've been writing an awful lot about that. We think that artificial intelligence that we're in the middle of, this is the fourth industrial revolution we're in the middle of. This is a really significant period of time.

If we went back in time, if we looked at the internet build-out, if we looked at it-- let's call it in the '90s-- if we looked at the television build-out in the '20s, if we looked at the railroad build-out of the 19th century, all of those had speculation involved in it and helped build an incredible amount of infrastructure and the systems that you and I benefit every day on.

So, I'm not saying, oh, all speculation is bad. What I'm saying is that confusing those two when you're an investor is that-- Typically I think what you need to do is take your net worth, put it on one piece of paper and then look at that and go, what is the money that I'm using that are for my long-term objectives? And what is the money that-- and then the other part of my money is going, what is what I call the bag of chips money, the money that if I lost it all, I'm all good with that?

And I think if you do those two things, clearly delineate the difference between those two-- don't kid yourself that, you know, being levered long triple Qs is an investment, but it's a speculation on a tech side of a market. And then understanding over here on your positioning and portfolio positioning.

There, create a wealth plan for yourself. Say, what is the cash needs that I immediately need to fund? What is the things that I want to invest in so that I can reduce the taxes and how the income that I'm deriving from my investments? What part is the investments am I using for the stuff I want to invest in five years from now? And then what is the stuff that I want to make sure that I'm investing for 10 years and longer out? And I think if you do those pieces, you're in really good shape.
