data.title: Royal Bank of Canada: Stellar Results, Limited Upside
data.description:
Thesis

Royal Bank of Canada (RY) is the largest bank in Canada and operates around the world in areas like personal banking, business banking, wealth management, insurance, and capital markets. When I last wrote about RBC in late August, I rated the stock as a Hold. The company’s core business looked very strong, with high returns on equity and solid capital levels. But the stock price already seemed expensive, and I didn’t see much room for it to go higher, especially since the benefits from the HSBC Canada deal and higher interest rates were starting to fade, while upcoming mortgage renewals and rising credit costs were becoming bigger concerns.

Since my call, RY has produced a total return of about 6.7%, which is just slightly better than the roughly 5.0% gain from the S&P 500 (SP500). Basically, the stock has mostly behaved the way I expected, but it's also broken out from a technical standpoint.

Keep in mind, I do recognize that the year-to-date results are strong, and the positive momentum is definitely working in RY’s favor.

That said, with the fourth-quarter results now out, my research shows that Royal Bank of Canada is operating at a very high level. The bank is delivering record profitability, has strong capital, and is showing real momentum in both Wealth Management and Capital Markets. But at the same time, the stock’s valuation already reflects most of that strength, leaving limited room for additional upside.

Royal Bank of Canada Performance Highlights

Royal Bank of Canada delivered one of the strongest years in its history. For the full year of 2025, it earned net income of $20.4 billion. That $20.4 billion was up 25% from the prior year. If you spread that profit across all the shares, you get diluted EPS of $14.07, also up 25%. Meanwhile, adjusted net income rose 20% to $20.9 billion and adjusted EPS increased 19% to $14.43 with return on equity moving up to 16.3% from 14.4%. And at the same time, the CET1 ratio, which is a key safety cushion regulators watch, stayed at 13.5%.

That level indicates the bank is still very well capitalized, even after returning $11.3 billion to shareholders through dividends and buybacks (see historical stats above).

Pre-provision, pre-tax earnings jumped 30% to $30 billion, the clearest evidence yet that the core earnings engine is firing on all cylinders. Higher net interest income came from solid Canadian banking volume growth and persistently attractive spreads, while fee-based revenue surged in Wealth Management on the back of strong market appreciation and continued net sales. Capital Markets also contributed meaningfully, with Global Markets and Corporate & Investment Banking both posting sharp revenue gains. The HSBC Canada deal folded in five extra months of results and is now fully absorbed, delivering the revenue bump they said it would without turning the cost base into a brushfire.

Every segment posted double-digit earnings growth except Commercial Banking, which still turned in a solid 7% despite eating higher provisions tied to rising unemployment, slower growth, and trade disruptions. Personal Banking was up 20%, Commercial 7%, Wealth Management 25%, Insurance 14%, and Capital Markets 18%.

The fourth quarter itself was a record-breaker. The bank earned $5.4 billion in net income and reported EPS of $3.76, with both numbers up 29% from a year earlier. Adjusted results were also strong, rising 25%.

For the full year, total revenue hit $66.6 billion, which is a 16% increase on a reported basis. Expenses went up too, but by a much smaller 7%, which helped create strong positive operating leverage.

Credit costs were higher, which is pretty normal at this stage of the economic cycle. For the full year, provisions for credit losses (PCL) came to $4.4 billion. The impaired loan ratio was 37 basis points, which is 9 basis points higher than last year, mainly due to issues in a few commercial industries.

Gross impaired loans increased to 83 basis points of total loans, up from 59 basis points, but the bank’s loan-loss reserves remain strong, and the extra provisions on still-performing loans were relatively small.

Liquidity remains extremely strong, with the Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) at 127%, giving the bank a large safety cushion. Royal Bank of Canada also raised its quarterly dividend by 6%, bringing it to $1.64. More importantly, it increased its medium-term return-on-equity (ROE) goal to 17% or higher for fiscal 2026.

Royal Bank of Canada Earnings Breakdown

What really jumps out to me in this report is how evenly Royal Bank of Canada’s earnings machine is running now. We saw that not every division, Personal Banking, Commercial Banking, Wealth Management, Insurance, and Capital Markets, is growing at the same speed, even so, together they’ve pushed the bank’s pre-provision, pre-tax earnings up 30% and they did it without relying on one big risky bet.

Personal Banking earnings were also up double digits, and Commercial Banking was lagging but still up. That’s pretty solid, especially considering OSFI has been warning again and again about higher unemployment, the risk of people renewing mortgages at tougher rates, and ongoing stress in commercial real estate across the system. In that environment, the fact that Canadian retail lending and commercial lending can still grow revenue while taking higher provisions for potential loan losses suggests the bank is being careful. It is not just pushing out loans to chase growth at any price.

To me, the bigger change is how much more important fee-based and market-related businesses have become for the bank. Wealth Management earnings rose 25%, helped by increases in fee-based client assets and strong net sales. RBC now oversees about $1.57 trillion in assets under management and roughly $5.6 trillion in assets under administration, both growing by double-digit percentages compared to last year.

Essentially, that positions the bank firmly in the global competition for asset and wealth management at a time when money continues to pour into ETFs and managed investment products. The U.S. ETF market alone is around $12.7 trillion in assets and has taken in about $950 billion in inflows so far this year. In practical terms, as that pool grows, a bank like Royal Bank of Canada can earn more steady fee income just by keeping and growing its share of client assets instead of relying only on interest spreads.

Capital Markets is, in my opinion, the other obvious growth driver. Full-year earnings for the segment were up 18%, and in Q4 alone, Capital Markets' net income surged more than 45% from a year earlier, thanks to stronger trading results and a comeback in M&A and underwriting activity. That matches what’s happening across the global investment-banking world, where fees are recovering, and advisory and equity capital markets revenues are growing at low double-digit rates as deal activity starts to pick up again.

RY’s Valuation

RY currently trades at 15.9× trailing non-GAAP earnings and 15.4× forward earnings, which is a 39% premium to the sector’s median valuation.

Its price-to-book ratio of 2.43× is even more extreme, about double that of its peers.

Seeking Alpha gives the stock a C- overall valuation grade, with D-level grades on almost every valuation metric except one: price-to-cash-flow, where it earns an A because the bank trades at only 3.45× cash flow. That looks cheap because banks throw off oceans of cash.

Analysts expect the P/E to come down to 14.4× in 2026 and 12.9× in 2027, based on modest 7.5–11% EPS growth.

Meanwhile, according to FAST Graphs' forecasting data, the fair value price target of C$216 basically implies no upside from where the stock trades today.

The valuation basically reflects a direct bet that management will hit its 17%+ return-on-equity target next year and keep it there. The HSBC acquisition costs are now fully absorbed, the bank’s AI tools are scaling, the business mix is shifting further toward high-margin Wealth and Capital Markets, and expense growth remains under control.

All of that seems reasonable, maybe even likely, as long as the Canadian economy keeps stumbling forward without slipping into a full recession.

But the safety cushion here is extremely thin. Revenue growth is expected to be only 5.4% going forward, compared to 7.2% for the sector, and net interest margins look set to dip again. Unemployment is still creeping up, and those commercial credit problems in autos and services probably aren’t going away anytime soon.

It would take just one quarter of stubbornly high expenses or one meaningful credit hit for the stock to get pushed back down to its old 12–13× earnings range. At this valuation, RBC gets zero credit for being the biggest Canadian bank.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’m sticking with my Hold rating on RY. The bank is performing extremely well, with real momentum in Wealth Management and Capital Markets, but at 15.9× trailing and 15.4× forward earnings, paired with only about 5.4% expected revenue growth and obvious credit and economic risks, I don’t see enough upside to justify paying such a high premium.

data.title: BBVA: The Risk-Reward Proposition Is Not Good For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
While I was bearish on Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria, S.A. (BBVA) about one year ago due to its sensitivity to rates, this has not played out as I was expecting, as the bank’s share price has more than doubled since then. The market has rewarded European banks for their somewhat aggressive capital return plans in recent quarters, and BBVA was no exception. Additionally, its failed attempt to buy its smaller Spanish competitor Banco Sabadell (OTCPK:BNDSY) was also well received by investors because it’s more likely that BBVA will return more capital to shareholders rather than seeking external growth in the near future.

Regarding its takeover offer for Banco Sabadell, after a relatively long period of regulatory review that took more than one year, the bank got approval to go ahead with its offer some months ago, but BBVA could not get the backing from most of Sabadell’s shareholders. Indeed, only about 25% of Sabadell’s shareholders accepted the offer, being below the 30% threshold required by the Spanish regulator. As the bank was not able to get at least 30%, the offer was automatically cancelled, and BBVA is not allowed to make a new offer for at least one year.

As BBVA is usually quite conservative regarding its M&A activity, I think it’s not much likely that BBVA will try again in the near future to buy Sabadell, but eventually, it may pursue a new takeover approach in the medium term if the opportunity arises and the price is attractive. Investors should note that BBVA had some talks about a potential acquisition back in 2020, but decided to walk away due to valuation issues, making the most recent attempt the second time it failed to acquire its smaller competitor.

As the Spanish banking market has become more concentrated over the past decade and there are nowadays a lower number of banks compared to ten or fifteen years ago, Sabadell is the most obvious target for a larger bank like BBVA or Banco Santander (SAN). This means BBVA could potentially try again to buy Sabadell in the future, but following this failed attempt, the bank announced a new share buyback program of €1 billion and announced an interim dividend of €0.32 per share, representing an increase of more than 10% from the previous interim dividend. This clearly shows that BBVA’s strategy has changed again to reward its shareholders instead of pursuing external growth, something that it’s not much likely to change in the near term.

From an income perspective, BBVA has distributed two dividends over the past year, for a total amount of €0.73 per share, leading to a dividend yield at its current share price of about 3.9%. This yield is much lower than it was in the past (it was yielding around 7% when I last analyzed it) due to a rising share price in recent years, making it less attractive from an income perspective than before. This is now below the European banking sector average dividend yield, which is around 4.9%, and also lower than compared to some of its peers, including Sabadell or Caixabank (OTCPK:CAIXY), showing that BBVA’s income appeal is not particularly impressive right now.

However, I think it’s quite likely that BBVA will increase its final dividend related to 2025 earnings by double-digits, as the bank’s operating income continues to show some resilience and the bank is well capitalized, allowing it to maintain an attractive capital return policy to shareholders. As can be seen in the next graph, BBVA’s CET1 ratio was 13.4% at the end of last September, being well above both its capital requirement and internal target range.

This means BBVA has an excess capital position and can distribute the vast majority of its earnings to shareholders, as the bank does not need to build up its capital position. Therefore, it’s not surprising to see that current estimates are for BBVA to distribute an annual dividend related to 2025 earnings of €0.82 per share, implying a final dividend of €0.50 per share. This represents an increase of 22% compared to its final dividend of €0.41 per share related to 2024 earnings, showing that BBVA is clearly committed to returning more capital to shareholders following its failed takeover attempt on Sabadell. At its current share price, this leads to a forward-dividend yield of about 4.35%, being nonetheless below average compared to other European banks and its closest peers. Its dividend payout ratio is expected to be less than 50%, which is clearly conservative and shows that BBVA’s dividend is sustainable over the long term.

From an operating perspective, as I was expecting when I last analyzed BBVA, the bank’s earnings have peaked in recent quarters and are now showing some weakness, a trend that is justified by its business model, that is considerably exposed to rates. Indeed, its net attributable profit was slightly above €2.5 billion during the third quarter of 2025 (Q3 2025), representing a quarterly decline of 8% and a drop of 3.7% on an annual basis.

This negative performance is justified by lower rates in Europe and Mexico over the past year, being a headwind to net interest income (NII), and also by higher operating expenses. BBVA is one of the European banks with a higher reliance on NII on total revenues, which was a major reason I was cautious about its financial performance at the end of last year. However, due to strong loan growth, the bank has been able to increase its NII in recent quarters, a better performance than I was expecting.

In Q3 2025, BBVA’s NII amounted to €6.6 billion, up by 13.2% YoY, a very good performance despite lower rates in Spain and Mexico. This was driven by strong activity and loan growth of 16% YoY, which was key to offset rate headwinds over the last year. Nevertheless, customer spreads were negatively affected in both countries, with a bigger impact in Spain as a large part of loans are set at variable rates.

However, interest rates are expected to be near or at the terminal rates in both geographies, which means pressure on customer spreads should ease ahead and, potentially, customer spreads may reverse over the coming quarters, supported by loan growth.

Regarding expenses, BBVA reported somewhat high expense growth in recent quarters, especially in Mexico and Turkey, due to inflationary pressures. This has pushed cost growth to 7.3% YoY in Q3 2025, being a higher rate than revenue growth and resulting in negative jaws, not being a positive development for the bank’s efficiency. Indeed, its cost-to-income ratio was close to 40% during the past quarter, while its target is to achieve a cost-to-income ratio around 35% in the medium term, something that may be achievable if loan growth continues strongly ahead and the bank can contain cost growth to moderate levels over the coming years.

Regarding asset quality, while there has been some deterioration in recent quarters, the bank’s overall credit quality remains solid and not much different from the recent past. This has been a better development than I was expecting some months ago, as the credit quality in the banking system has remained resilient despite several external shocks for BBVA, such as the U.S. trade war or economic instability in Turkey. As shown in the next graph, its cost of risk ratio is much higher than compared to other European banks, which usually report cost of risk ratios below 0.5%, but it’s closer to its peer Banco Santander and remains at acceptable levels for a bank that has a large exposure to emerging markets.

While its net profit was down in the last quarter, and earnings growth may be relatively low ahead due to cost pressures that may offset some revenue growth driven by its loan book expansion, the market continues to focus on capital returns and has pushed banking valuations to relatively high levels. BBVA has been no exception and is currently trading at 1.85x book value, being the highest valuation over the last decade by a wide margin, as shown in the next graph.

This shows that BBVA’s strong share price performance in recent months has been driven by a re-rating of its shares rather than being supported by earnings growth, something that I don’t see as sustainable for long. Investors should keep in mind that the banking sector is cyclical and valuations are, historically, mean reverting throughout the economic cycle. This means that current high P/BV multiples may not be sustainable if economic conditions deteriorate ahead. Moreover, this valuation is also clearly at a premium to its peers, given that the sector’s average is only around 1.2x book value, being another sign of overvaluation.

Conclusion

BBVA’s strong share price rally in recent months has been driven by a higher valuation multiple, pushing its P/BV multiple to levels that I was not clearly expecting a year ago. While the bank’s operating performance has been better than I was expecting and credit losses have remained at acceptable levels, the bank is not reporting earnings growth, and this is not likely to change much in the near term, making me cautious about the sustainability of its valuation. In my opinion, the risk-reward proposition for long-term investors is not great, and its income appeal is also lower than it was in the past, and, therefore, I maintain my ‘sell’ recommendation as I see its valuation has been too high compared to its own history and its peers.
